key_insights.insight: Consistent sleep-wake timing is more crucial for longevity than sleep duration.
key_insights.quote: It is the degree to which you stabilize when you go to bed and when you wake up that predicts mortality.

key_insights.insight: Circadian disruption, even from being awake a few hours late a couple of times a week, significantly elevates the risk of various diseases.
key_insights.quote: If you're awake for two hours between 10 p.m. and 4 a.m. for two days per week for 25 days of the year, you qualify as a kind of card carrying shift worker and you likely have significant circadian disruption.

key_insights.insight: Consolidating your eating window, regardless of food quality, significantly improves metabolic health.
key_insights.quote: If we can just consolidate our eating window to eight to 10 hours, we get ourselves like 60% of the way to our kind of metabolic goals.

key_insights.insight: Viewing light between 10 p.m. and 4 a.m. has a pro-depressive effect.
key_insights.quote: If you're viewing light between the hours of, again, this is kind of this magic window of time, 10 p.m. and 4 a.m., it actually has a pro-depressive effect.

key_insights.insight: Sleep debt in leaders negatively impacts the psychological safety of their team.
key_insights.quote: For every 45 minutes of sleep that the leader had, there was a significant decrease in psychological safety of the direct reports.

key_insights.insight: Social jet lag increases non-suicidal ideation, especially in vulnerable individuals.
key_insights.quote: For every hour of variability between week, day, and weekend schedules, they saw a 17% increase in non-suicidal ideation.

key_insights.insight: Sex with a partner, but not masturbation, before bed is linked to better sleep and recovery markers.
key_insights.quote: People who are reporting that they're having sex before bed...with a partner have better markers of sleep and recovery... We don't see the same effect when folks are having sex without a partner and reporting it.

key_insights.insight: High heart rate variability is correlated with better adaptation to environmental stress, and is modifiable to a degree.
key_insights.quote: Heart variability is kind of a measure of that ability to adapt to your environment in a functional way.

actionable_advice.advice: Stabilize sleep-wake time by waking up at the same time every day and getting as much light as possible in the morning.
actionable_advice.how_to: Set a consistent alarm, even on weekends. Spend 5-20 minutes outdoors in natural light upon waking. If waking before sunrise, use bright artificial lights to mimic daylight.

actionable_advice.advice: Consolidate your eating window to within 10 hours and avoid eating 2-3 hours before sleep.
actionable_advice.how_to: Choose a 10-hour window for eating (e.g., 7 a.m. to 5 p.m.) and stick to it. Avoid snacking or meals after your designated eating window closes.

actionable_advice.advice: Deploy "mini moments of deactivation" throughout the day using the physiological sigh.
actionable_advice.how_to: Practice the double inhale followed by an extended exhale (physiological sigh) 5-10 times throughout the day, especially during stressful moments, to activate the parasympathetic nervous system.

actionable_advice.advice: Dim your home environment and minimize artificial light exposure after sunset.
actionable_advice.how_to: Use dimmer switches, turn off unnecessary lights, and use blue light blocking glasses and filters on screens in the evening.

memorable_stories: Kristen's personal story of growing up with a mother struggling with alcoholism and how it has fueled her passion for human flourishing and enabling people to reach their full potential.
Kristen's anecdotal experience with collegiate athletes, where regular sleep-wake times correlated with academic performance.
Whoop's research demonstrating sleep debt correlation with a direct report's feeling of psychological safety during team meetings.
contrarian_claims: The concept of chronotypes (being a 'night owl' or 'morning person') is largely 'total BS', and that most people can adjust their sleep-wake times.
Sleep regularity is more predictive of all-cause mortality than sleep duration, challenging the conventional focus solely on getting enough hours of sleep.
episode_title: The Diary Of A CEO with Steven Bartlett - The Fitness Scientist: "Even A Little Alcohol Is Hurting Your Health!", "Late Night Screen Time Linked To Cancer!", "Working Shifts Kills You 15 Years Early!" - Kristen Holmes
